questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: For a particle travelling at a relativistic speed $v$, the magnitude of its linear momentum is proportional to
questions.solution:
The relativistic linear momentum of a particle of rest mass $m_0$ moving with velocity $v$ is given by

$p = \dfrac{m_0 v}{\sqrt{1 - v^2/c^2}}$

Since $m_0$ is a constant, the magnitude of linear momentum is proportional to $\dfrac{v}{\sqrt{1 - v^2/c^2}}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When the speed of a particle increases, its rest mass
questions.solution:
The rest mass of a particle is an intrinsic property and is independent of its speed or the frame of reference.

While the relativistic mass of a particle increases with speed according to the relation $m = \dfrac{m_0}{\sqrt{1 - \dfrac{v^2}{c^2}}}$, its rest mass $m_0$ remains constant.

Therefore, when the speed of a particle increases, its rest mass remains the same.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
The length of a rod is measured by an experimenter. Initially, both the experimenter and the rod are stationary with respect to the lab. Evaluate the following statements:
(A) If the rod begins to travel parallel to its length while the observer remains stationary, the measured length will decrease.
(B) If the rod remains stationary but the observer begins to travel parallel to the measured length of the rod, the length will decrease.
options: Both statements A and B are true.
Statement A is true, but statement B is false.
Statement B is true, but statement A is false.
Both statements A and B are false.
questions.solution:
The proper length of the rod is measured when both the rod and the observer are at rest relative to each other. Let this length be $L_0$.

According to the special theory of relativity, when there is a relative velocity $v$ between the rod and the observer parallel to the length of the rod, the measured length $L$ is given by the length contraction formula $L = L_0 \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{v^2}{c^2}}$.

In statement A, the rod moves with respect to the stationary observer, creating a relative velocity. Thus, the measured length decreases.

In statement B, the observer moves with respect to the stationary rod, which also creates a relative velocity. Thus, the measured length decreases as well.

Therefore, both statements A and B are true.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An experimenter measures the length of a rod. In the scenarios provided, all motions are relative to the lab and parallel to the rod's length. In which scenario will the measured length be minimum?
options: The rod travels at speed $v$ but the experimenter remains at rest.
The rod and the experimenter travel in the same direction at the same speed $v$.
The rod remains at rest but the experimenter travels at speed $v$.
The rod and the experimenter travel in opposite directions at the same speed $v$.
questions.solution:
The measured length of a rod is given by $L = L_0 \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{u^2}{c^2}}$, where $L_0$ is the proper length and $u$ is the relative speed between the rod and the observer.

The measured length is minimum when the relative speed $u$ is maximum.

In scenario (1), the relative speed is $u = v$.

In scenario (2), the relative speed is $u = 0$.

In scenario (3), the relative speed is $u = v$.

In scenario (4), the rod and the experimenter move in opposite directions. The relative speed is given by the relativistic velocity addition formula:

$u = \dfrac{v + v}{1 + \dfrac{v^2}{c^2}} = \dfrac{2v}{1 + \dfrac{v^2}{c^2}}$

Since $u > v$ for any $v > 0$, the relative speed is maximum in this scenario.

Thus, the measured length is minimum when the rod and the experimenter travel in opposite directions at the same speed $v$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When the speed of a particle travelling at a relativistic speed is doubled, its linear momentum will
questions.solution:
The relativistic momentum of a particle is given by $p = \dfrac{mv}{\sqrt{1 - \dfrac{v^2}{c^2}}}$

When the speed is $v$, the initial momentum is $p_1 = \dfrac{mv}{\sqrt{1 - \dfrac{v^2}{c^2}}}$

When the speed is doubled to $2v$, the new momentum is $p_2 = \dfrac{m(2v)}{\sqrt{1 - \dfrac{4v^2}{c^2}}}$

Comparing $p_2$ with $2p_1$:

$2p_1 = \dfrac{2mv}{\sqrt{1 - \dfrac{v^2}{c^2}}}$

Since $1 - \dfrac{4v^2}{c^2} < 1 - \dfrac{v^2}{c^2}$, it follows that $\sqrt{1 - \dfrac{4v^2}{c^2}} < \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{v^2}{c^2}}$

Therefore, $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 - \dfrac{4v^2}{c^2}}} > \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 - \dfrac{v^2}{c^2}}}$

This implies $p_2 > 2p_1$

Thus, the linear momentum becomes more than double.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When a constant force is applied to a particle, its acceleration will
questions.solution:
According to Newton's second law of motion, the force applied on a particle is given by $F = ma$, where $m$ is the mass of the particle and $a$ is its acceleration.

Since the mass of a particle is constant, a constant force $F$ will produce a constant acceleration $a = \dfrac{F}{m}$.

Therefore, the acceleration of the particle will remain constant.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When a charged particle is projected at an extremely high speed perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field, it will
options: travel along a curve with a decreasing radius of curvature
travel along a curve with an increasing radius of curvature
questions.solution:
When a charged particle moves in a uniform magnetic field perpendicular to its velocity, it experiences a magnetic force that provides the necessary centripetal acceleration for circular motion.

At extremely high speeds, the accelerating charged particle continuously radiates electromagnetic energy in the form of synchrotron radiation.

Due to this continuous loss of energy, the kinetic energy and the momentum $p$ of the particle decrease over time.

The radius of curvature of the path is given by $r = \dfrac{p}{qB}$.

Since the momentum $p$ decreases while the charge $q$ and magnetic field $B$ remain constant, the radius of curvature $r$ also decreases.

Therefore, the particle travels along a curve with a decreasing radius of curvature.
